summary: McKenzie argues that the Heartbleed vulnerability's unprecedented rapid response was largely due to superior marketing: a memorable name, professional logo, dedicated website, and clear writing. He contrasts this with previous critical vulnerabilities like CVE-2013-0156 (Rails YAML deserialization) that failed to spur action because they were communicated through obtuse CVE numbers and plain-text mailing list posts. The post breaks down exactly why each marketing element worked — the name was emotionally evocative and technically accurate, the website copy was tight enough for executives to grasp in 30 seconds, and the logo conveyed danger and professionalism. McKenzie concludes that the open source community's cultural resistance to marketing is actively harmful, because getting people to patch systems is itself an engineering problem.
key_points: Memorable names like 'Heartbleed' drive action where CVE numbers fail — technologists couldn't even get VPs to approve remediation for the Rails YAML vulnerability because the name communicated no urgency
The Heartbleed announcement was masterful technical writing: accurate yet comprehensible to non-technologists, leading with severity and business impact rather than burying it in technical details
A dedicated web presence with a logo and clear copy creates a canonical, citable, authoritative reference that plain-text emails on Google Groups never can

The OSS community's cultural disdain for marketing is counterproductive — if critical patches don't get adopted because the communication was obtuse, the adversaries win regardless of engineering quality

Marketing is an engineering discipline: convincing hundreds of thousands of people to act immediately on a vulnerability is a coordination problem that requires the same rigor as writing the patch itself
money_quotes: We were all racing against for loops and the prize for 2nd place was 'Our customers' security gets horribly abused.'
This makes marketing an engineering discipline. We have to get good at it, or we will fail ourselves, our stakeholders, our community, and the wider world.
Marketing is just a tool, and it can be used in the cause of truth and justice, too.

The engineering reality of their compromises cannot be thwarted by effort or the feeling of self-righteousness we get by not getting our hands dirty with marketing, it can only be thwarted by successfully patched systems.
Data gets weighed by the gigabyte but business critical documents spur immediate action when threatened.
Man, would that have been an easier month if we had all been talking about DeserialKiller.
If we get an A for Good Effort but do not actually achieve adoption because we stick to our usual 'Put up an obtuse notice on a server in the middle of nowhere' game plan, the adversaries win.
The Heartbleed logo is probably one of the highest ROI uses of ~$200 in the history of software security.
key_insight: Getting people to patch critical vulnerabilities is a coordination problem that demands the same engineering rigor as writing the fix itself — memorable names, clear writing, and professional branding aren't vanity, they're the delivery mechanism for security.
